---
title: "1.14 Calling Instance Methods"
subtitle: "Part 2: Methods with Arguments"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Methods Calls with Arguments

### Turtle methods can take values

In the last lessons, we used simple **methods** like `forward` and `turnRight` to make the turtle draw lines.

You may have noticed that `forward()` and `backward()` always move the same number of pixels (`100` pixels), and `turnRight()` and `turnLeft()` always turn at right angles (`90` degrees).

This is a little limiting.

## Methods Calls with Arguments

### More flexible movement

What if we wanted to draw a triangle or the letter A?

These require smaller angles to draw diagonal lines and different length lines.

Luckily, there are more complex methods in the `Turtle` class that let you specify the number of pixels to move forward or the number of degrees to turn.

## Arguments and Parameters

### Values passed to methods

These values that you can give to methods to help them do their job are called **arguments** or **parameters**.

For example, we can give the argument `200` in `forward(200)` to make the turtle go forward `200` pixels instead of the default of `100`.

We can give the argument `30` in `turn(30)` to make the turtle turn `30` degrees instead of `90` degrees.

## Source Note

### Method calls with arguments

`object.method(arguments);` is used to call an object's method and give it some argument values that it will need to do its job.

```java
yertle.forward(200);
yertle.turn(30);
```

The object receiver is `yertle`.

The arguments are `200` and `30`.

## Parameters vs. Arguments

### Method definition and method call

When you create your own method, the variables you define for it are called **parameters**.

When you call the method to do its job, you give or pass in **arguments** to it that are then saved in the parameter variables.

In the definition of the `forward` method, it has a parameter variable called `pixels`.

In the call to `forward(200)`, the argument is the value `200`, which will get saved in the parameter variable `pixels`.

## Parameters and Arguments Figure

### Source diagram

You will learn to write your own methods in Unit 3.

In this unit, you will learn to call methods that are already written for you.

![Method signatures with parameters and method call arguments](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-14-calling-instance-methods/method-param-arg.png){fig-align="center" width="64%"}

## Vocabulary Check

### `dragndrop:: methodVocab`

Drag the definition from the left and drop it on the correct word on the right.

| Definition | Word |
|---|---|
| an object's behaviors or functions that can be used or called to do its job | methods |
| the values or data passed to an object's method | arguments |
| the variables in a method's definition that hold the arguments | parameters |
| asking to run the method | method call |

## Turtle Class Diagram

### Methods inherited from `SimpleTurtle`

Here is the `Turtle` class diagram again that shows some of the variables and methods inherited from the `SimpleTurtle` class in the class `Turtle` that are written for you.

![Turtle class diagram](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-14-calling-instance-methods/turtleUMLClassDiagram.png){fig-align="center" width="54%"}

## Overloaded Instance Methods

### Same name, different signature

Methods are said to be **overloaded** when there are multiple methods with the same name but a different **method signature**, where it requires a different number or type of parameters.

For example, we have two different `forward` methods:

```java
yertle.forward();
yertle.forward(100);
```

The arguments given to the method need to correspond to the order and types of the parameters in the method signature.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: TurtleTestMethods1`

Can you make `yertle` draw a square and change the pen color for each side of the square?

Try something like:

```java
yertle.setColor(Color.red);
```

This uses the `Color` class in Java, which has some colors predefined like red, yellow, blue, magenta, cyan.

You can also use more specific methods like `setPenColor`, `setBodyColor`, and `setShellColor`.

## Coding Exercise

### Triangle task

Can you draw a triangle?

The `turnRight()` method always does `90` degree turns, but you'll need external angles of `120` degree for an equilateral triangle.

Use the `turn` method which has a parameter for the angle of the turn in degrees.

For example, `turn(90)` is the same as `turnRight()`.

Try drawing a triangle with different colors.

## Starter Code

### `TurtleTestMethods1`

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TurtleTestMethods1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World world = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle yertle = new Turtle(world);

        yertle.forward(100);
        yertle.turnLeft();
        yertle.forward(75);

        // TODO: add method calls for a square, a triangle, and colors

        world.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### Preserved Runestone checks

Runestone checks that the code changed from the starter.

It also checks:

::: {.tight-list}
- changes color at least `3` times by counting `Color(`
- calls `.turn` at least `3` times
- calls `.turn(` at least `1` time
- calls `.forward(` at least `4` times
:::

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: DrawAHouse`

![Simple house](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-14-calling-instance-methods/house.png){fig-align="center" width="24%"}

The following code uses a turtle to draw a simple house, but the lines are mixed up.

Drag the code blocks to the right and put them in the correct order to first draw a square for the house and then a red triangle for the roof.

You can type this code in the Active Code window above to see it in action.

## Mixed Blocks

### Setup blocks

```java
// Block 1
public class TurtleDrawHouse

// Block 2
{

// Block 3
public static void main(String[] args)
{

// Block 4
World world = new World(300,300);

// Block 5
Turtle builder = new Turtle(world);
```

## Mixed Blocks

### Square block

```java
// Block 6
// Draw a square
builder.turnRight();
builder.forward(100);
builder.turnRight();
builder.forward(100);
builder.turnRight();
builder.forward(100);
builder.turnRight();
builder.forward(100);
```

## Mixed Blocks

### `DrawAHouse` continued

```java
// Block 7
builder.setColor(Color.red);

// Block 8
// Draw a triangle
builder.turn(30);
builder.forward(100);
builder.turn(120);
builder.forward(100);
builder.turn(120);
builder.forward(100);

// Block 9
world.show(true);

// Block 10
}

// Block 11
}
```

## Correct Order

### `DrawAHouse`

::: {.code-scroll .compact}
```java
public class TurtleDrawHouse
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World world = new World(300,300);
        Turtle builder = new Turtle(world);

        // Draw a square
        builder.turnRight();
        builder.forward(100);
        builder.turnRight();
        builder.forward(100);
        builder.turnRight();
        builder.forward(100);
        builder.turnRight();
        builder.forward(100);

        builder.setColor(Color.red);

        // Draw a triangle
        builder.turn(30);
        builder.forward(100);
        builder.turn(120);
        builder.forward(100);
        builder.turn(120);
        builder.forward(100);

        world.show(true);
    }
}
```
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- call an object method with `object.method(arguments)`
- distinguish parameters in a method definition from arguments in a method call
- match method arguments to the number, order, and type in the method signature
- explain method overloading using `forward()` and `forward(100)`
- use turtle methods with arguments for distance, turn angle, and color changes
- reorder turtle code so setup comes before drawing and `world.show(true)` comes last
:::

## End

### 1.14 Part 2 complete

Part 3 continues with methods that return values.
